Subject Details: The prototype of the modern torpedo was first developed in the 1860s. Germany, Britain, and Italy used torpedoes, and later Japan, in World War I. The U.S. had a working torpedo in 1870 and modified it until 1915; however, the development slowed during World War I, and it wasn't until post-war that the U.S. had a modern era torpedo.

Subject: Smooth bore rotating cannon
Subject Details: A smoothbore weapon has a smooth inside barrel without rifling and fires projectiles without significant spin. The standard infantry arm was the smoothbore musket in the 18th century, with rifled muskets becoming the norm until the middle of the 19th century after the introduction of the Minié ball. The U.S. adopted rifled small arms and artillery during the American Civil War; however, the heavy coastal artillery used Rodman smoothbores until 1900, as newer coastal defense systems like Parrott rifles were still unreliable.

Subject: Electrocuting enemy
Subject Details: Electrocuting one's enemy in trench warfare was not present during WWI; however, more countries leveraged technology with electric components by WWII. J.J. Duffie of California patented a trench warfare system in 1917 that involves charging the air over and in the trench with an electric conducting substance and then causing a high current to flash through the atmosphere in the trench, incapacitating the man. However, there does not seem to be widespread practice of this invention, nor much information on whether the U.S. military used it.

Projectiles like the one Marcianus Rossi designed and patented in 1917, which involved a hollow projectile with cutters inside that could be discharged from the gun and cut through the wires when fired, were submitted but were not widely implemented or successful. Subject: Modified Lewis range finder
Proposed By: U.S. Rapid Fire Gun and Power Co.
Subject Details: The Lewis rangefinder was a successful invention developed by Colonel Isaac Newton Lewis and was adopted by the War Department in 1896. Seventeen years later, he attempted to improve this range and position finders and ended up with the primary model, excluding all others and saving the government money. He also interestingly designed the Lewis Gun, in 1911, which was the first light machine gun used during WWI. Even though it was designed in the United States, it was not adopted by the U.S. military first because of political differences between Lewis and General William Crozier. Lewis ended up leaving the U.S. Army.
Proposer Details: The U.S. Rapid Fire Gun and Power Co. acquired the Driggs-Seabury Weapons plant in Derby after 1904. Interestingly, they are proposing this modified Lewis Range finder as far back as 1904, when they had not started making these guns and had acquired this weapons plant.

Subject: Wire-cutting projectile
Subject Details: Wire obstacles became prominent in WWI and WWII, used to entangle troops and tactically guard specific areas. A wire-cutting projectile is likely a technology that effectively cuts through these new wire obstacles efficiently. Projectiles like the one Marcianus Rossi designed and patented in 1917, which involved a hollow projectile with cutters inside that could be discharged from the gun and cut through the wires when fired, were submitted but were not widely implemented or successful. More successful methods of overcoming barbed wire included the later development of tanks.

Subject: Caseless cartridge
Subject Details: This invention attempts to eliminate the cartridge case holding the material for the weapon in order to reduce the weight and cost of ammunition. An early type of caseless ammunition was first developed in the 18050s and never saw widespread acceptance. During WWII, the Germans had some success in modernizing the technology, but Japan ultimately developed an aircraft that used internal propellant caseless ammunition. This technology was replaced with external-propellant caseless ammunition, which didn't appear in production until 1968.

Subject: Hollow bullet
Subject Details: A hollow-point bullet expands upon impact with a soft target so that more of the projectile's energy is transported into the target over a smaller distance. Soft leaf bullets would often deform and provide some expansion when they hit a target at a high velocity. At the Hague Convention of 1899, these bullets that can easily expand or flatten were banned; however, the U.S. did not agree to this convention and could use these bullets in warfare, although they did employ a convention to not create arms that cause "unnecessary suffering". Despite the ban in the military, they are some of the most common bullets used by civilians, largely due to the fact that they reduce the risk of bystanders being hit.

A fully automatic gun will fire continuously as long as the trigger is depressed and there is ammunition left in the chamber. The mid-1800s, with the invention of the Gatling gun, was the first time a practical weapon could be reloaded relatively quickly. The development of the Maxim machine gun was the first standard automatic gun used in large-scale and modernized warfare.

Subject Details: Dynamite was the first safely manageable explosive stronger than black powder, created in 1866. TNT and dynamite are very different explosives in that TNT is the high explosive that was adopted primarily by the military, while dynamite shells have been less popular because they degenerate quickly under severe conditions and can be triggered by stray fire too easily. The German forces started using TNT as a filling for shells in 1902.

Subject: Portable telephone line
Subject Details: The British used the first field telephones in the Second Boer War and there was widespread use by WWI. A field telephone operated over wire lines and could operate point-to-point or from a switchboard. The wires needed to be buried in the ground and set up in order for the phones to work.

Subject: Submarine mine
Subject Details: An explosive weapon placed in water to damage or destroy submarines. Mines were used extensively during the Russo-Japanese War of 1904-1905 to destroy ships. During WWI, mines were used to defend coasts, ports, and shipping lanes around the globe. During WWII when the usage of the U-boats increased, mine technology developed in order to destroy them more effectively.

Subject: Range finder
Subject Details: A rangefinder is a device used to measure distances to remote objects and to survey an artillery target's range. WWII seemed to be the first significant innovation jump in this technology, using two telescopes focusing on one target but apart on the baseline after the late 1890s.

Subject: Torpedo tube
Subject Details: As torpedo technology advanced, ships and submarines would have to be equipped with torpedo tubes.

Subject: Electric cannon
Subject Details: An electric cannon was developed by the U.S. for helicopter gunships after 1967. There seems to be little progress on electric cannon inventions that made it to the battlefield until the 1960s.

Subject: Entrenching tool
Subject Details: A tool used for digging into earth-like substances. The U.S. Army introduced the trowel bayonet in 1870, which could be used by soldiers as a weapon and an entrenching tool.
